# Transcrição: 2. Correct Funnel Audience Exclusion & Targeting Setup (post iOS14)

## [00:00 - 03:02]

So what I want you to take away from this video is first of all how I structure my campaigns, my ad campaigns, but more importantly why I've had to change some of my audiences based on what's happened with iOS 14 in particular. So forgive my quality of my drawings if it doesn't quite come out. I'm using my drawing pad here. So typical funnel, the way I structure it is I have my cold traffic at the top. Cold for me is people that haven't clicked or engaged. Then I have my warm. So these are people that have engaged but not clicked. Who are they? For example, it could be fans. It could even be your email list if you're doing it Legion outside of your website. People that have engaged in your page or posts and things like that. Video viewers that have seen a video end up going into warm for me at least. And then I have hot. They are the ones that are pretty much primed and ready to buy. These are people that have visited the sites, product page, cart page, blog page. Those kind of people they've been on the site. They've had a touch point with the product I'm selling. And then I have my existing users. This is the one that most people actually forget to market to. You can actually market to people that have already bought. So they're the full stages of my funnel. Now, if we look at how I would set this up, when I'm targeting my cold audience, I would generally exclude my warm, hot, and existing audiences. So that means I exclude existing customers, people that have visited the site, and then people that have engaged so that I know that people at the top of funnel are absolutely cold for warm as above, except I exclude hot and existing. And then here I exclude existing. Now with the hot funnel in particular, and this can also happen in warm as well, there may be a case where you know, you're breaking down your funnel based on which part of the funnel they've got to so typical kind of view of retargeting, for example. And by the way, there'll be some of you that you'll be saying, you know, retargeting hasn't been working as well, and things like that. And what I've identified from working on a numerous, numerous accounts since iOS 14 has kicked in is that I think a lot of people haven't quite understood how aggregated events manager work. So aggregated events, just to remind you, it's eight events that you prioritize from top to bottom. So you say this is high priority. This is low priority. And let's say for ecom, for example, you have purchase, you have payment info, initiate checkout, add to cart, view content, and then maybe something else. So that's five of your eight permitted items on the list. Now, when you kind of look at this,

## [03:04 - 06:07]

I think part of the misunderstanding has been, what does it actually mean and how does it work? What it means is if someone opts out of iOS 14, iOS tracking, so let's just say iOS, because 14, 15, 16 doesn't matter, it's just going to be there now. If someone opts out of tracking, it doesn't mean that none of these can be tracked. What you're saying to Facebook is, and this is Facebook's actually message to you is if you could track one event, which one's the most important for you. So for example, let's say I'm running a webinar, and I'm looking at the important events that I want to track. I might also have purchase here. I might also have, initiate checkout. But here, maybe I have my replay page. Maybe I have my live page. Maybe I have my registration pixel. Maybe I have my lead. In both cases, what I'm saying to Facebook is if you can only give me one pixel, the one at the top is the one I'd love for you to record. Now, what happens is if someone visits your site on iOS 14 device, and they're just walking through the site, walking through and then make a purchase, this is what gets recorded by Facebook. So Facebook says someone converted here. And in your ads manager, you'll see one purchase. You'll see zero for everything else. And you could look at that and say, hey, what's going on? This is just estimated or something. It's because of aggregated events manager that you've told Facebook purchases my most important pixel if I can only choose one. If the user doesn't purchase, and they get to initiate checkout, for example, then what happens is Facebook will not record view content or add to cart. It will just tell you an initiate checkout occurred. Obviously, no purchase payment info or purchase. So your reporting shows one initiate checkout. This causes no end of problem. I'll give you an example. One of my rules for e-com and automation relied on looking at the cost per add to cart at a certain time in the day. And if it wasn't met, then the ad would be paused. Guess what? It doesn't work right now because there may be some really good performance and good CPAs, but there's zero add to cart, maybe not zero, but there's a very low number of add to cart. So I could look at this and I could look at my add to cart cost. And let's say my target is below $20. And I know generally I'm going to get a good CPA. I might still have a really good CPA, but now my cost per add to cart has gone to $40, because Facebook's not reporting as many of those conversions on the one side on a reporting side. It's important to understand what's actually happening. And this is why some of our decision making and optimizations has changed. And that's why some of yours also needs to change. But that's not the only problem here. The other problem is if you think about how people are some some people

## [06:07 - 09:12]

build their funnels, you might have, for example, let's say people that have viewed content in the last seven days, people that have added to cart in the last seven days. And that's pretty much it. That's a very standard way of setting up campaigns, or you might have, you know, eight to 14 for view content and the same for add to cart. Now, back in the day, when things were good, that would kind of work. It would work, you know, if someone's added to cart, someone's view content that end up in this audience, whichever one's relevant for what they've done. Now, if you go back to what I just explained to you, you could end up having 1000 people visit your product page. You could end up with more people actually going to initiate checkout than add to cart. That doesn't make sense. But that's what the reporting will tell you because of aggregated events managers. So what happens with your retargeting? All of a sudden, where this was matching, you know, let's say the majority of the 1000 people that landed, that would dramatically drop. Why is that? Because not you don't have as many people firing this pixel, because you've told Facebook, if a higher priority pixel event happens, track me that event. What that does is, it puts a note in as manager and says, Hey, there's one here here. And it also identifies that person as reaching that step. So you can remark it to them at that particular step. So if they if, let's say majority people do add to cart, you your audience is here, will have more people than you have view content. For example, so what that all of a sudden means is where view content, let's say from those 1000 people, let's just assume pre iOS, you could target 1000 people. Now, and this is just a rough guess. Maybe you can only target 600 people. And maybe the edge to cut to cart, let's say, you know, 20% added to cart, where before you could target 200, maybe you can only target 50. And maybe and this is the problem here. How do you then reach the other people that have gone into your cart? Or now you're going to have to also have zero to seven initiate checkout zero to seven purchase payment info. The reason is, prior to iOS 14, if you targeted people that added to cart, by default, you would have included people that had viewed content. If you'd targeted people who had added to cart, not purchased, you would have automatically included people that have initiate checkout and payment info as well. But what happens now is people may have fired that initiate checkout, but not fired that add to cart. And that's why when we set up our audiences, we're now including initiate checkout and payment info

## [09:12 - 11:38]

in our remarketing audiences and in our excludes through the funnel as well. So here, for example, in the past, just excluding view content might have been in fact, let's go further up. So in our warm and cold, just excluding view content would have by default excluded anyone else that's got further as well, because by virtue of them landing on the site, they fired the view content pixel. So if I just exclude people here that have viewed content, I've also excluded the rest prior to iOS 14. However, now, if I only exclude view content, there's no guarantee I'm going to exclude these people as well, because there are cases where view content doesn't fire, but add to cart does or view content and add to cart don't fire initiate checkout does. So now we have to exclude every single pixel event separately to ensure that I'm excluding everyone that's potentially visited my site. And the same occurs with remarketing. So rather than have a zero to seven day add to cart, for example, if I'm trying to target all checkout abundance, previously just having add to cart would work. But now I have to have add to cart plus initiate checkout plus payment info, and then excluding purchases. And by the way, when you exclude, exclude via email plus pixel, because the pixel is less reliable, you might not get all those hits, email, hopefully you'll cover off more of those as well. So this will hopefully get you to open your eyes and see the opportunities on how to get your remarketing working better again, and also how to improve your excludes further up the funnel. So hopefully this gives you a strong base to be able to improve your targeting because essentially when you've got the funnel set up properly and everything's working, you can target the right user the right out of the right time. So for example, someone that knows your brand, you know, maybe there's users generated content, maybe there's testimonials, someone that's never heard of your brand, not clicked, not engaged, your ads are going to be different. And one of the powerful parts of Facebook advertising prior to iOS 14 was being able to execute that. But you still can to some level as long as you get your understanding right on exactly how to do that and what's available to you as well.
